message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  Many countries have passed their first COVID-19 epidemic peak. Traditional epidemiological models describe this as a result of non-pharmaceutical interventions that pushed the growth rate below the recovery rate. In this new phase of the pandemic many countries show an almost linear growth of confirmed cases for extended time-periods. This new containment regime is hard to explain by traditional models where infection numbers either grow explosively until herd immunity is reached, or the epidemic is completely suppressed (zero new cases). Here we offer an explanation of this puzzling observation based on the structure of contact networks. We show that for any given transmission rate there exists a critical number of social contacts,
                  <jats:italic>
                    D
                    <jats:sub>c</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  , below which linear growth and low infection prevalence must occur. Above
                  <jats:italic>
                    D
                    <jats:sub>c</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  traditional epidemiological dynamics takes place, as e.g. in SIR-type models. When calibrating our corresponding model to empirical estimates of the transmission rate and the number of days being contagious, we find
                  <jats:italic>
                    D
                    <jats:sub>c</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  ~ 7.2. Assuming realistic contact networks with a degree of about 5, and assuming that lockdown measures would reduce that to household-size (about 2.5), we reproduce actual infection curves with a remarkable precision, without fitting or fine-tuning of parameters. In particular we compare the US and Austria, as examples for one country that initially did not impose measures and one that responded with a severe lockdown early on. Our findings question the applicability of standard compartmental models to describe the COVID-19 containment phase. The probability to observe linear growth in these is practically zero.
                </jats:p>
title: Why are most COVID-19 infection curves linear?

reference.unstructured: Bailey NTJ (1975) The Mathematical Theory of Infectious Diseases and its Applications. (Hafner Press, New York).
Riou J , Althaus CL (2020) Pattern of early human-to-human transmission of Wuhan 2019 novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV), December 2019 to January 2020. Euro Surveillance 25 (4).
Ogris G , Oberhuber F (2020) Spread of SARS-CoV-2 in Austria. https://www.sora.at/fileadmin/downloads/projekte/Austria_Spread_of_SARS-CoV-2_Study_Report.pdf
Gudbjartsson DF , et al. (2020) Spread of SARS-CoV-2 in the Icelandic Population. The New England Journal of Medicine April 14.
Infections database at CSSE at Johns Hopkins University. https://github.com/CSSEGISandData/COVID-19/tree/master/csse_covid_19_data/csse_covid_19_time_series
Anderson RM , May RM (1991) Infectious Diseases of Humans. (Oxford University Press, Oxford).

reference.article-title: The spread of epidemic disease on networks
Epidemic outbreaks in complex heterogeneous networks

reference.article-title: Temporal dynamics in viral shedding and transmissibility of COVID-19
